exemplar.title: To what extent are different genders of Kolej MARA Banting students aware of the impact of improper disposal of packaging for E-commerce towards plastic waste pollution, measured by knowledge, attitudes, and practices?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent are different genders of Kolej MARA Banting students aware of the impact of improper disposal of packaging for E-commerce towards plastic waste pollution, measured by knowledge, attitudes, and practices?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Follows the conventional IA structure throughout
Maintains a generally logical flow from data to interpretation
weaknesses: Formatting glitches and overly long digressions disrupt reader engagement
Verbose wording and occasional contradictions hinder concision
Dense tables and paragraphs reduce coherence and clarity in places
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and organization are sustained but imperfect (band 2), terminology use and concision meet only one aspect (band 2), and logic/coherence is mainly sound yet difficult to follow in parts (band 2). The combined performance justifies a score of 2.

strengths: Formulates a focused, coherent research question specifying population, comparison and KAP dimensions
Provides strong contextualisation with global and Malaysian plasticâwaste statistics
Clearly outlines the environmental issue relevant to the research question
weaknesses: Phrasing of the research question is overly verbose
Explanation of how awareness translates to measurable waste reduction is brief
Connections between awareness and wasteâreduction behaviors could be more deeply explored
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments show topâband performance in question formulation and contextualisation (band 5), with a slightly lower performance in explaining connections (band 4). The overall performance aligns with the descriptor for 5 marks: a coherent, focused research question and strong environmental context, albeit with some depth missing in linking awareness to outcomes.

strengths: Designs a repeatable survey method capable of collecting relevant quantitative data
Describes a quota sampling strategy appropriate for representing student subgroups
Outlines basic ethical considerations such as confidentiality and informed consent
weaknesses: Method details (question order, software settings, significance level) are insufficient for full reproducibility
Sampling justification is superficial and ignores potential biases
Ethical section omits data storage security, withdrawal rights and researcher bias management
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method design meets band 3â4 but lacks robustness (band 4), while sampling strategy and ethics both occupy band 3. The combined performance aligns with a holistic score of 3: an appropriate but only moderately justified and outlined plan.

strengths: Evaluates conclusion using an external study and identifies relevant weaknesses in research design
Describes multiple strengths and limitations of the method (e.g. Likert scale advantages and sample size issues)
Suggests meaningful modifications and further research (mixed methods, longitudinal follow-up)
weaknesses: Discussion of environmental implications and policy impact is limited
Fails to address pilot-test robustness and sampling bias mitigation
Improvement suggestions are relevant but not prioritised or deeply linked to specific flaws
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand ratings indicate a band 3â4 performance: evaluation of conclusions and methods with omissions (band 3), clear description of strengths/weaknesses (band 4), and meaningful but not fully significant improvements (band 3). Overall, a score of 4 reflects solid evaluative thinking with room for deeper analysis.

strengths: Presents all raw data in clear tables and converts them into appropriate charts with error bars
Performs complete and correct analysis (means, SDs, independent t-test) identifying key patterns
Analyzes data fully, showing all trends and linking them back to research aims
weaknesses: Minor calculation ambiguities (summation notation, p-value threshold typo)
Some charts lack axis labels or direct percentage annotations
Conclusion statement contains inconsistencies regarding hypothesis acceptance
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation and analysis both fulfil topâband descriptors (band 5), with a nearâtop conclusion drawing that is supported by data but slightly confused in its interpretation. The overall performance merits 5 marks.

strengths: Proposes a solution (greenârewards programme) directly based on study findings
Justification clearly links consumer behaviour insights to programme benefits
Application fully meets the highestâband descriptor for proposal quality
weaknesses: Evaluation of the solution considers only voluntary uptake risk
Omits analysis of programme costs, scalability and monitoring mechanisms
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The proposal is well justified and directly derived from the results (band 3), while the evaluation covers some but not all relevant strengths and weaknesses (band 2). The overall performance aligns with the top mark of 3 in Applications.

strandSelections.reasoning: Terminology such as âindependent variableâ, âquota samplingâ, âstandard deviationâ is consistently used, yet verbosity and redundant wording reduce concision.  Either appropriate terminology or concision is achieved; hence band 2.

The conclusion is compared with one external study and several weaknesses (limited variables, response honesty) are noted, showing some evaluative thinking.  Still, the discussion is brief and does not explore broader environmental implications in depth, so evaluation is present but incomplete.

The student draws a data-based conclusion (males appear more aware) and acknowledges the non-significant p-value, but then inconsistently states that the hypothesis is rejected while still claiming males are more aware.  Support from data is present yet partially confused, preventing full validity required for band 5-6.

Means, standard deviations and an independent two-sample t-test are correctly calculated and interpreted.  Patterns (higher male means, lower female SDs) are identified and linked back to research aims.  Analysis is mathematically sound and complete, satisfying the âcorrectly and completelyâ criterion.

Suggested modifications (add variables, longitudinal follow-up, larger sample, mixed methods) are relevant and would address some weaknesses, but they are broadly stated and not prioritised or linked to specific methodological flaws.  They are therefore âmeaningfulâ rather than âsignificantâ.

The survey procedure, variables table, scoring system and statistical test provide a repeatable blueprint capable of collecting relevant quantitative data.  Nevertheless, some details are vague (exact questions for all 20 items, order of operations, software settings) and sample size justification is weak, so the method is appropriate but not yet robust enough for the highest band.

Links between the gender-related awareness study and the plastic-waste issue are described (e.g. hypothesis paragraph and rationale for gender focus).  However, the explanation is brief and misses deeper pathways (e.g. how awareness translates into measurable waste reduction), so the connections are described rather than fully and comprehensively explained.

The student formulates a single, explicit question, clearly specifying population (Kolej MARA Banting students), comparison (male versus female), focus (awareness) and dimensions (knowledge, attitudes, practices).  Although slightly wordy, it remains sufficiently narrow to be investigated through one instrument (a survey) and is internally coherent, satisfying the descriptor for a focused question.

The student notes one strength (mutual benefit) and one key weakness (voluntary uptake) but omits other relevant aspects (costs, monitoring, scalability).  This meets the descriptor for âevaluates some relevant strengths and weaknessesâ (2 marks).

The report follows the conventional IA structure (RQ, introduction, method, results, discussion) but occasional formatting glitches and long digressions disrupt flow.  Thus structure is evident but not sustained flawlessly, fitting band 2.

Strengths (quick data collection, Likert advantages) and weaknesses (neutral midpoint, small sample, distribution bias) are clearly described plus at least one improvement (larger sample using Krejcie & Morgan).  Most but not all limitations are covered, matching the middle bandâs descriptor.

The introduction contextualises the study within the escalating problem of plasticâwaste pollution from e-commerce, quoting global and Malaysian statistics and explaining why public awareness is critical.  The environmental issue is therefore discussed, not merely outlined, and is clearly relevant to the question.

Quota sampling is named and its purpose stated, but the justification is surface-level ("accurate representation of KMB students") and ignores obvious limitations (self-selection, online distribution bias).  Thus the sampling strategy is described, fulfilling band 3-4, but not convincingly justified for band 5-6.

The student lists two ethical considerations (confidentiality, informed consent) and indicates that no sensitive data are collected.  Physical risks are negligible for an online survey.  While covered, the discussion lacks depth on data storage, withdrawal rights and researcher bias, therefore meeting âoutlinesâ rather than âdescribes comprehensivelyâ.

All raw data are organised in extensive tables and converted into clear bar/pie charts for every question and section; error bars are included on comparative graphs.  Presentation is appropriate, accurate and covers the full data set, matching the top-band descriptor.

The green-rewards programme is directly derived from findings on consumer behaviour and is justified through benefits to both consumers and companies, aligning fully with the highest APP band for proposal.

Arguments generally progress logically from data to interpretation, but occasional contradictions (p-value interpretation) and overlong paragraphs hinder reader comprehension in places.  The work is mainly logical but difficult to follow at times, matching band 2.

annotations.comment: Data tables in Sections BâD are comprehensive but extremely dense. Consider summarising key patterns graphically to enhance reader comprehension.
The research question is clear and specifies population, comparison, and KAP dimensions, but the phrasing is overly verbose. Consider tightening wording for concision while maintaining focus.
Strong contextualisation of global and Malaysian plasticâwaste statistics convincingly situates the studyâs environmental issue.
Explanation of how awareness translates into actual wasteâreduction behaviors is brief. Elaborate on behavioral pathways linking KAP scores to measurable outcomes.
The hypothesis states a directional expectation but omits specification of significance level or statistical test. Include p-value threshold to strengthen planning.
The variables table fixes all respondents at age 19, which is unrealistic and ignores withinâgroup variation. Justify or broaden age grouping.
Procedures section lacks detail on question order, deployment timing, and survey administration steps. Elaborate for full reproducibility.
Justification for questionâsection categories is superficial. Explain why specific item counts (e.g. 7 knowledge items) reliably capture each KAP construct.
Pilotâtest description omits sample size, duration, and feedback use. Include these details to demonstrate thorough risk and ethics planning.
Sampling strategy names quota sampling but lacks justification of subgroup choices and bias control. Provide rationale and limitations.
Ethical section omits data storage security, withdrawal rights, and researcher bias. Outline comprehensive risk assessment and confidentiality measures.
Presentation of Likert scale value table is clear and exhaustive, covering all response categories. Excellent visual organisation.
Standard deviation formula uses Î£f rather than Î£(xâÎ¼)Â² directly; clarify summation over frequencies and values for correctness.
Example mean calculation shows numerator grouping ambiguity; ensure parentheses and summation bounds are clearly typeset in LaTeX.
Table 4 on page 10 lacks explicit mention of statistical test applied to demographic percentages. Consider adding chi-square test or note in legend.
Gender composition figure lacks axis labels or percentages annotated directly; add these for clearer data interpretation.
Mean and SD table for Section B accurately summarises central tendencies, facilitating clear comparison between genders.
General trends list effectively highlights key findings but omits comment on statistical significance from t-test. Reference p-values for completeness.
Threshold for p-value is incorrectly stated as 0.5 rather than 0.05. Adjust criterion to standard Î± = 0.05 in statistical analysis section.
The conclusion states the hypothesis is rejected yet acknowledges null hypothesis acceptanceâthis inconsistency undermines validity. Clarify final decision.
External study from Selangor strengthens conclusion evaluation. Good use of context to support IA findings.
Evaluation omits broader environmental implications of findings. Discuss how genderâtailored interventions could impact waste management at institutional and policy levels.
Method evaluation identifies strengths and limitations of Likert scale but omits discussion of pilotâtest robustness and sampling bias mitigation.
Solution proposal for green rewards is directly tied to findings, offering justified application based on consumer behaviour insights.
Solution evaluation notes uptake risk but misses analysis of program costs, scalability, and monitoring mechanisms. Expand weaknesses discussion.
Report structure follows IA conventions but page transitions include lengthy tables disrupting reader flow. Consider summarising key points per page.
Technical terminology such as âtwo-sample t-testâ and âstandard deviationâ is used accurately, demonstrating strong disciplineâspecific language.
Overlong paragraphs in Discussion hinder coherenceâsplit into subâsections or bullet points to improve logic and reader engagement.
